questions.question: What makes a gate 'transversal'?

options.text: It operates faster than other gates
It applies the same operation to each physical qubit independently
It uses entanglement between code blocks

questions.explanation: A transversal gate applies the same single-qubit gate to each physical qubit of the code block (or corresponding pairs for two-qubit gates). This is automatically fault-tolerant because an error on one qubit cannot spread to others.

questions.question: Which gates are transversal for the Steane code but NOT for the surface code?
questions.explanation: The Steane code (and color codes) support transversal Hadamard and Phase gates due to their self-dual structure. Surface codes do not—they require code deformation or lattice surgery for these gates.

questions.question: What is gate teleportation used for?

options.text: Implementing gates that aren't transversal using pre-prepared ancilla states

questions.explanation: Gate teleportation implements non-transversal gates by using a pre-prepared ancilla state |U⟩ = U|+⟩. A CNOT and measurement 'teleport' the gate action to the data qubit, with only Clifford corrections needed afterward.

questions.question: What is the purpose of magic state distillation?

options.text: To create entanglement between code blocks
To convert noisy magic states into fewer but higher-fidelity magic states

questions.explanation: Magic state distillation takes multiple noisy |T⟩ states and uses error detection to produce fewer, cleaner |T⟩ states. The 15-to-1 protocol, for example, uses 15 noisy states to make 1 state with cubic error reduction.

questions.question: Why is the T gate particularly expensive in fault-tolerant computation?

options.text: No code supports transversal T, so it requires magic state injection and distillation
It causes more errors than other gates

questions.explanation: Due to the Eastin-Knill theorem, no code can have transversal gates for all required operations including T. The T gate requires magic state injection, and preparing high-fidelity magic states through distillation is resource-intensive.

questions.question: What does the Eastin-Knill theorem state?

options.text: All quantum gates can be made transversal
No quantum error-correcting code can have a universal set of transversal gates
Only Clifford gates are useful for computation

questions.explanation: The Eastin-Knill theorem proves that no quantum code can have transversal implementations of a universal gate set. This is why at least one gate (typically T) must use non-transversal methods like magic state injection.

questions.question: After gate teleportation for the T gate, the correction (if measurement is 1) requires applying a non-Clifford gate.
questions.explanation: False. After T gate teleportation, the correction (if measurement outcome is 1) is S†Z, which is purely Clifford. This is the key insight—the non-Clifford gate is 'absorbed' into the magic state, and only Clifford corrections are needed.
